metadata.summary: Three-tier token architecture (primitive, semantic, component) via W3C DTCG JSON and Style Dictionary for multiplatform consistency.

content.title: Design Tokens Fundamentals
content.summary: Three-tier token architecture (primitive, semantic, component) keeps single source of truth via W3C DTCG JSON and Style Dictionary. Semantic naming enables theming without component changes. Purpose-named tokens survive dark mode and theme swaps.

why.p: Ad-hoc CSS variables and Tailwind configs create designer-developer drift: values in Figma diverge from code within one sprint. Three-tier token architecture with a CI contrast-lint step keeps a single source of truth across web, iOS, Android, and visionOS surfaces. Semantic naming makes dark/high-contrast mode swaps possible without touching component code.

list.item: Bootstrapping a design system targeting two or more platforms.
Migrating from ad-hoc CSS variables to a single source of truth.
Adding dark/light/high-contrast modes to a shipped product.
Designer-developer round-trip workflows where Figma values drift from code.
Multi-brand white-label products that need theme swaps without rebuild.
Single-page MVP with one designer/developer — overhead exceeds value until month 3+.
Static two-page marketing site — Tailwind defaults suffice.
Throwaway internal tools with no design-language target.
Teams that haven't agreed on semantic naming yet — resolve naming first.

rule.statement: Organize tokens in exactly three tiers: primitive (raw values) → semantic (purpose-named aliases) → component (component-scoped). Components must reference only semantic tokens, never primitive tokens directly.
rule.rationale: Components that reference primitive tokens (e.g., color.blue.500) make dark-mode and theme swaps impossible without touching every component. Semantic layer is the indirection point that makes theming work.

section.name: tier-definitions
section.p: Primitive: raw values with no semantic meaning. Examples: color.blue.500 = #3B82F6, spacing.4 = 16px. These are the only tier that ever contains actual values.
Semantic: aliases that give meaning to primitives. Examples: color.text.default references color.gray.900; color.surface.primary references color.white. Names express purpose, not appearance.

Component: aliases scoped to a specific component. Examples: button.background references color.surface.primary; card.padding references spacing.md. Generate only for components that actually need them.

section.name: w3c-dtcg-json-schema
section.p: Emit tokens in W3C Design Tokens Community Group format. Each token uses $value (not value) and $type. References use {token.path} syntax.

example.note: Uses $value and $type per current DTCG spec. Semantic tokens reference primitives via {path} syntax.

section.name: benefits
section.p: Consistency: identical values across web, iOS, Android, visionOS from a single JSON source. Scalability: update once, regenerate all platform outputs via Style Dictionary. Theming: dark/light/high-contrast modes require only semantic-layer overrides, no component changes. Maintenance: single source of truth with a publishable changelog. Collaboration: shared naming vocabulary eliminates designer-developer value drift.

section.name: core-rules

rule.statement: Name semantic tokens by purpose, not appearance. Use color.text.muted, not color.gray.600.
rule.rationale: Appearance-based names make theming impossible — color.gray.600 cannot be a dark-mode override because the name describes the light-mode hue. Purpose-based names survive theme changes because the purpose stays constant.

rule.statement: Pair every semantic foreground token with its background token. Lint every text/background pair for WCAG 2.2 AA contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text) in CI.
rule.rationale: Contrast violations are the most common accessibility failure in design systems. Catching them at token-generation time costs seconds; catching them in a11y audit costs sprints.

rule.statement: Limit token nesting depth to four levels maximum (e.g., color.button.primary.hover). Enforce in CI.
rule.rationale: LLMs generate tokens with 6–8 nesting levels (color.button.primary.hover.background.disabled). At that depth, naming collapses — no human can find any token in a library of 1,500+ without semantic search.

rule.statement: Treat token rename as a breaking change. Version token packages with SemVer. Ship a migration codemod with every rename.
rule.rationale: Consumers depend on token name stability. A silent rename of color.text.default to color.text.primary breaks every downstream consumer silently — CSS variables go undefined, not errored.

section.reference: CI script: fail if semantic text/background pair < 4.5:1

antipattern.description: Primitive tokens leaking into component code (e.g., color.blue.500 used in a button component directly).
antipattern.reason: Makes theming and dark mode impossible. Any change to blue.500 breaks every component referencing it unpredictably.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated tokens skipping the semantic layer entirely — outputting only primitive and component tiers.
antipattern.reason: Without semantic layer, theming requires editing every component token. Force 3-tier output schema in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Auto-deriving dark theme algorithmically from light theme.
antipattern.reason: Algorithmic inversion produces poor contrast and violates accessibility requirements. Dark-mode semantic layer must be hand-tuned and contrast-validated.

antipattern.description: Cross-platform output emitting px values for iOS (expects pt) or Android (expects dp/sp).
antipattern.reason: LLMs paste px everywhere without transforms. Style Dictionary must declare unit transforms per platform or assets render at wrong sizes on mobile.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use agents to (a) generate the initial 3-tier token JSON (primitive → semantic → component) from a design brief, (b) lint token files for naming and reference-cycle violations, (c) generate platform outputs (CSS variables, iOS/Android resources, Tailwind config) via Style Dictionary, and (d) audit existing CSS for hardcoded values that should be tokenized. Human review for naming and the semantic layer is mandatory — names outlive everything.

Agents can generate initial 3-tier token JSON from a design brief, lint token files for naming and reference-cycle violations, generate platform outputs (CSS variables, iOS/Android resources, Tailwind config) via Style Dictionary, audit existing CSS for hardcoded values to tokenize. Human review for naming and the semantic layer is mandatory — names outlive the codebase.

Gotchas: W3C DTCG schema uses $value, not value — agents may emit obsolete shape. Hex/OKLCH conversions produce subtly off colors silently — assert exact round-trip match. Generated dark themes need manual contrast verification — never accept agent-declared "accessible".

section.name: limitations
section.p: Token sprawl: teams reach 1,500+ tokens and can't find any of them; without governance, naming collapses. Pure primitive layer leaks into components ("color.blue.500" everywhere) and theming becomes impossible. Cross-platform output diverges silently — fonts, line-heights, and elevation translate poorly to iOS/Android. Figma Variables ↔ JSON sync is still fragile; renaming on either side breaks the other. Animation, motion, and shadow tokens are weakly standardized — most teams hand-roll them.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.note: CLI tools: style-dictionary (transform tokens to platform outputs), Tokens Studio CLI (sync Figma Variables ↔ JSON), theo (Salesforce token transformer), cosmiconfig (centralize token config), chromatic (visual regression on token-driven components), axe-cli (a11y contrast check).

Services: Tokens Studio (Figma, SaaS + plugin, agent-friendly JSON export), Specify (SaaS, REST + CLI), Supernova (SaaS, REST), Knapsack (SaaS, partial agent-friendly), Style Dictionary (OSS, CLI/JS), Figma Variables (SaaS, partial REST API beta).

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.note: Generate W3C-format token JSON from design brief (brand, primary_palette, modes, scale, type_scale, components_in_scope). Output 3 layers: primitive (raw values, no semantic names), semantic (alias only, contrast-paired), component (only for listed components). Validate WCAG 2.2 AA contrast for every text/background pair in semantic layer. Emit DTCG (W3C Design Tokens Community Group) JSON.
Audit CSS for hardcoded values that should be tokens. Output JSONL: {file, line, value, suggested_token, reason}. Skip values inside @keyframes, transition timing, and z-index.

template.purpose: W3C DTCG 3-tier token skeleton (primitive / semantic / component).
CI script: fail if any semantic text/background pair is below WCAG AA 4.5:1.
